past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, well controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 6 years ago, managed with oral agents
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 71
Mother alive at 82 with Alzheimer's disease
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disorders

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 12 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 2 miles three times per week

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Mildly inattentive, makes occasional errors on serial sevens. Language fluent but with intermittent word-finding pauses. Follows three-step commands. Recall 2/3 objects at 5 minutes. Fund of knowledge intact. Judgment and insight preserved.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. Mild left lower facial droop noted. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 deltoid, 4/5 biceps, 4/5 triceps, 4+/5 grip, 4/5 interossei. Left lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Pronator drift present on the left. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. Proprioception intact at the great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at the ankles bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 3+ with an equivocal plantar response on the left. Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric on the left, intact on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No rapid alternating movement abnormality.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently. Mild left arm swing reduction. Slight left leg circumduction. No Romberg sign.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. No signs of increased intracranial pressure clinically. One witnessed focal seizure involving left arm tonic-clonic activity lasting approximately 90 seconds with secondary generalization, prompting ED evaluation.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headaches, new left arm weakness, and a first-time seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Robert Dalton is a 58-year-old right-handed man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. His wife reports that over the past three weeks, he has been complaining of progressively worsening headaches, predominantly on the right side, which are worst in the early morning and occasionally wake him from sleep. He has been using over-the-counter ibuprofen with only marginal relief. She also noticed that about two weeks ago, he began having difficulty using his left hand at work, dropping tools and struggling with fine motor tasks such as buttoning his shirt. She describes him as being somewhat more forgetful and 'not himself' over the past ten days, occasionally repeating questions and seeming confused about appointments. This morning at approximately 07:15, while eating breakfast, his wife observed his left arm begin to shake rhythmically, followed within thirty seconds by his eyes rolling back and a generalized convulsion lasting approximately ninety seconds. He was postictal and confused for about fifteen minutes afterward. EMS was called and he was transported to the emergency department. On arrival, he was alert and conversant but had a persistent mild headache. He denies any prior seizure history, recent head trauma, febrile illness, vision changes, nausea, or vomiting. He has not had any recent weight loss or night sweats. His wife reports no recent travel or sick contacts. A non-contrast CT scan obtained in the emergency department revealed a large hyperdense mass in the right frontal lobe with surrounding hypodensity consistent with edema and approximately 7 mm of midline shift to the left. He was admitted to the neurology service for further workup.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha, better organized over the left hemisphere
background.symmetry: Significant asymmetry with attenuation and slowing over the right frontotemporal region
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates with eye opening bilaterally

findings.morphology: Phase-reversing sharp waves with aftergoing slow wave

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to concern for elevated intracranial pressure

eeg.impression: Background asymmetry with attenuation and slowing over the right hemisphere. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure.

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontal lobe, centered in the precentral and middle frontal gyri, extending to involve the subcortical white matter and corona radiata

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense with areas of intrinsic T1 hyperintensity suggesting hemorrhage or proteinaceous material
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous with mixed signal, central T2 hyperintensity indicating necrosis or cystic change
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive perilesional FLAIR hyperintensity extending into the right frontal white matter, corona radiata, and across the genu of the corpus callosum
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim indicating high cellularity; central areas show facilitated diffusion
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Thick irregular ring enhancement with nodular enhancing components along the medial and posterior margins

findings.mass_effect: Effacement of the right frontal horn of the lateral ventricle. Approximately 8 mm left midline shift of the septum pellucidum. Mild compression of the right caudate head. Sulcal effacement over the right frontal convexity.
findings.borders: Irregular and infiltrative margins, indistinct interface with adjacent brain parenchyma

findings.location: Right frontal lobe white matter, extending into the ipsilateral corona radiata and genu of corpus callosum

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in edematous regions

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to overall mass effect and midline shift
findings.borders: Finger-like projections along white matter tracts

volumetrics.lesion_volume: Approximately 54 cm³ including enhancing and necrotic components
volumetrics.edema_volume: Approximately 82 cm³ of perilesional FLAIR signal abnormality

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified elsewhere in the brain
Mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes in the periventricular white matter bilaterally
mri.impression: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontal mass with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, surrounding vasogenic edema, and significant midline shift. The lesion involves the precentral gyrus and extends toward the corpus callosum. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 80 bpm with left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria, likely related to long-standing hypertension. No acute ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hematocrit 38.5 % (L), Sodium 152 mEq/L (H), Glucose 129 mg/dL (H), PT 13.6 seconds (H), HbA1c 7.2 % (H).

findings.region: Enhancing rim of the right frontal mass
findings.signal: rCBV_ratio: 3.8 (reference: normal brain = 1.0; high-grade glioma threshold > 1.75); rCBF: Elevated in enhancing components; Ktrans: Elevated, indicating blood-brain barrier disruption

findings.region: Central necrotic core of the mass

output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 3.8) in the enhancing rim of the right frontal mass, strongly supporting a high-grade glial neoplasm. The central necrotic core shows expected low perfusion. Perilesional perfusion abnormalities suggest infiltrative tumor beyond the contrast-enhancing boundary. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Enhancing component of right frontal mass
findings.signal: choline: Markedly elevated (Cho/Cr ratio 3.6; normal < 1.5); NAA: Markedly reduced (NAA/Cr ratio 0.4; normal > 1.5); Cho_NAA_ratio: 9.0 (markedly elevated, consistent with high-grade tumor); lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks present, indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism; myoinositol: Mildly elevated
findings.description: Abnormal metabolite ratios consistent with high-grade neoplasm

findings.region: FLAIR-hyperintense region adjacent to enhancing mass
findings.signal: choline: Mildly elevated (Cho/Cr ratio 1.8); NAA: Mildly reduced (NAA/Cr ratio 1.1); lipid_lactate: Absent

output.impression: Perilesional spectral changes suggest infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: Normal size and enhancement bilaterally, simple 8 mm cyst right kidney
Degenerative changes lumbar spine, no suspicious lytic or blastic lesions

Histopathology.value: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation with glomeruloid bodies, and areas of palisading necrosis

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Microscopic description Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation with glomeruloid bodies, and areas of palisading necrosis N/A (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 35% % (H), IDH1/IDH2 mutation Wild-type (negative) N/A (H), MGMT promoter methylation Unmethylated N/A (H), TERT promoter mutation Present N/A (H), EGFR amplification Present N/A (H), +7/-10 chromosome Present N/A (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Microscopic description: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (12 mitoses per 10 HPF), microvascular proliferation with glomeruloid bodies, and areas of palisading necrosis N/A (H)

findings.signal: left_hand_activation: BOLD activation identified in the right precentral gyrus, partially displaced posteriorly and medially by the mass. The activation focus for left hand motor function is located approximately 8 mm from the posterior-medial enhancing margin of the tumor.; right_hand_activation: Normal BOLD activation in the left precentral gyrus, anatomically preserved; foot_activation: Bilateral paracentral lobule activation, normal position

Broca_area: Normal BOLD activation in the left inferior frontal gyrus (pars opercularis and triangularis); Wernicke_area: Normal BOLD activation in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus; laterality_index: 0.85 (strongly left-lateralized for language)

output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates that the right frontal mass displaces the primary motor cortex posteriorly and medially. The patient is left-hemisphere dominant for language, and language areas are in the contralateral hemisphere and not at direct surgical risk. DTI tractography shows displacement but apparent preservation of the right corticospinal tract. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: glioblastoma standard treatment protocol Stupp regimen

results.title: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.key_finding: Concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide with radiotherapy significantly improved median survival (14.6 vs 12.1 months) and 2-year survival (26.5% vs 10.4%) compared to radiotherapy alone

results.title: Effect of tumor-treating fields plus maintenance temozolomide vs maintenance temozolomide alone on survival in patients with glioblastoma: a randomized clinical trial
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.key_finding: Addition of tumor-treating fields (TTFields) to maintenance temozolomide improved median overall survival (20.9 vs 16.0 months) in newly diagnosed glioblastoma

results.title: MGMT gene silencing and benefit from temozolomide in glioblastoma
results.authors: Hegi ME, Diserens AC, Gorlia T, et al.
results.key_finding: MGMT promoter methylation is a predictive biomarker for benefit from temozolomide; unmethylated patients derive less benefit but temozolomide is still included in standard of care

output.summary: The current standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma is maximal safe surgical resection followed by the Stupp protocol: concurrent radiation therapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) with daily temozolomide (75 mg/m²), followed by 6 cycles of adjuvant temozolomide (150-200 mg/m², days 1-5 of 28-day cycle). Tumor-treating fields (TTFields/Optune) are recommended as an addition to maintenance temozolomide. MGMT promoter methylation status guides prognosis but does not currently change first-line therapy. For patients with unmethylated MGMT, the standard Stupp protocol remains recommended, though clinical trial enrollment should be considered.

interactions: Metformin + Dexamethasone: Corticosteroids antagonize hypoglycemic effect; monitor blood glucose closely, may need to increase metformin dose or add insulin
Ibuprofen + Dexamethasone: Increased risk of GI bleeding and peptic ulceration; consider discontinuing ibuprofen and adding a proton pump inhibitor
warnings: Monitor blood glucose at least every 6 hours during dexamethasone therapy given known diabetes
Consider GI prophylaxis with proton pump inhibitor
Taper dexamethasone to lowest effective dose as soon as clinically feasible to minimize steroid side effects
Monitor for steroid-induced hyperglycemia, steroid myopathy, and insomnia
alternatives: Methylprednisolone as alternative corticosteroid, though dexamethasone is preferred in neuro-oncology due to minimal mineralocorticoid activity

differential.key_features: Single lesion without known primary cancer; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis negative; irregular infiltrative margins more suggestive of primary brain tumor than metastasis
No fever, normal WBC, no recent infection or immunocompromise; restricted diffusion at the rim rather than center argues against abscess
Immunocompetent patient; necrosis and heterogeneous enhancement atypical for lymphoma; not periventricular
Age and sex atypical; no MS history; closed ring enhancement and significant mass effect argue against demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent CT head to evaluate for mass lesion and assess for herniation risk
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large right frontal hyperdense mass with surrounding edema and midline shift

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 10 mg IV loading dose followed by 4 mg IV every 6 hours to reduce peritumoral edema and mass effect
Start levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily for seizure prophylaxis given presenting seizure
Order MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast to characterize the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, surrounding edema, and midline shift consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG to characterize epileptiform activity and guide antiepileptic management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right frontal focal slowing with epileptiform discharges in peri-tumoral cortex

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, liver function tests, and HbA1c
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC, coagulation, and hepatic function; mildly elevated glucose consistent with known diabetes

optimal_actions.action: Request MR perfusion imaging to assess tumor vascularity and grade
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in enhancing rim consistent with high-grade tumor

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out primary malignancy elsewhere (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of primary extracranial malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for stereotactic biopsy or maximal safe resection
Request functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Motor cortex displaced by tumor, language lateralized to left hemisphere

optimal_actions.action: Following tissue diagnosis, plan Stupp protocol: concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy + temozolomide) followed by adjuvant temozolomide
Adjust diabetic medications given dexamethasone-induced hyperglycemia; monitor glucose closely and consider insulin sliding scale
Add proton pump inhibitor for GI prophylaxis while on dexamethasone

critical_actions: Initiate dexamethasone to reduce peritumoral edema and prevent herniation
Start antiepileptic medication (levetiracetam) given presenting seizure
Obtain MRI brain with contrast to characterize the mass
Rule out metastatic disease with systemic imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis)
Obtain tissue diagnosis via neurosurgical biopsy or resection
Monitor blood glucose closely given dexamethasone use in a diabetic patient
Plan for Stupp protocol (concurrent chemoradiation followed by adjuvant temozolomide) after tissue diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture in the setting of a large mass with significant midline shift (herniation risk)
Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue diagnosis
Delaying corticosteroids in a patient with significant mass effect and midline shift
Administering anticoagulation without clear indication given upcoming surgical planning
Starting temozolomide prior to tissue confirmation of diagnosis
key_reasoning_points: The combination of progressive headaches worst in the morning, new focal neurological deficits (left arm weakness), and new-onset seizure in a middle-aged adult is a classic presentation of a brain tumor requiring urgent imaging
CT showing a large enhancing mass with midline shift mandates immediate dexamethasone for cerebral edema and neurosurgical consultation
MRI features of heterogeneous ring enhancement with central necrosis, thick irregular rim, and surrounding edema are characteristic of glioblastoma
Metastasis workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) is necessary even when imaging is highly suggestive of primary brain tumor to guide treatment planning
Tissue diagnosis is essential before initiating definitive therapy; molecular markers (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) guide prognosis and treatment decisions
EEG confirms the structural basis for seizures and guides antiepileptic drug management
Dexamethasone will worsen glycemic control in this diabetic patient, requiring proactive glucose management
